findings.description: Comprehensive README with excellent structure covering all essential sections: installation, usage, documentation links, contributing guidelines, and examples. Includes badges for build status, npm version, and license.

findings.recommendation: Maintain current quality. Consider adding a Prerequisites section to explicitly state Node.js version requirements.

findings.description: React uses a hybrid documentation strategy with external comprehensive documentation site (react.dev) for API reference, tutorials, and guides. The repository includes package-specific READMEs for individual modules.
findings.evidence: Main README references multiple documentation sections at react.dev: Quick Start (react.dev/learn), Tutorial (react.dev/learn/tutorial-tic-tac-toe), API Reference (react.dev/reference/react), Advanced Guides (react.dev/learn/escape-hatches). Package-level READMEs found: packages/react-devtools-shell/README.md, packages/react-server/README.md, packages/react-devtools-inline/README.md. Flow types present (.flowconfig and flow-typed/ directory visible in repository tree).
findings.recommendation: Continue leveraging external documentation for detailed API reference. Consider adding a lightweight API overview in-repo for offline quick reference.

findings.description: Exceptional changelog with comprehensive version history from 2013 to 2025, following best practices with clear formatting, categorization, contributor attribution, and migration guidance.
findings.evidence: CHANGELOG.md (265,863 bytes, SHA: 5953fafc278638db7932f59e28555c2d52a80a06) contains releases from v19.2.1 (Dec 3, 2025) back to v0.3.0 (2013). Each release includes: date, categorized changes (React Core, React DOM, React Server Components, etc.), contributor GitHub handles (e.g., @sebmarkbage, @gaearon), PR/issue links for traceability (#35277), breaking changes sections, and links to migration guides (e.g., 'Read the React 19 upgrade guide').
findings.recommendation: Maintain current exemplary changelog practices. This is a reference implementation for open-source projects.

findings.description: Strategic use of external documentation (react.dev) provides superior user experience with search, versioning, and interactive examples compared to markdown-only approach.
findings.evidence: Main README acts as navigation hub with 15+ links to react.dev sections: Installation (react.dev/learn/installation), Describing the UI (react.dev/learn/describing-the-ui), Adding Interactivity (react.dev/learn/adding-interactivity), Managing State (react.dev/learn/managing-state), API Reference (react.dev/reference/react), Where to Get Support (react.dev/community). README states 'You can find the React documentation on the website' and 'The documentation is divided into several sections'.
findings.recommendation: Continue current hybrid approach. For a library of React's scale, external documentation is the correct architectural choice. Ensure react.dev remains synchronized with repository releases.

findings.description: Documentation is highly discoverable with clear navigation from README to all resources. Multiple entry points for different user personas (learners, developers, contributors).
findings.evidence: README provides distinct pathways: 'Quick Start' for beginners (react.dev/learn), 'Add React to an Existing Project' for integration scenarios (react.dev/learn/add-react-to-an-existing-project), 'Create a New React App' for new projects (react.dev/learn/start-a-new-react-project), Contributing section with external guide link, Good First Issues link (github.com/facebook/react/labels/good%20first%20issue), and Community support link (react.dev/community).
findings.recommendation: Excellent current state. Consider adding a 'Documentation' or 'Resources' section to README to consolidate all documentation links in one place.

findings.description: Clear versioning with detailed release notes and migration guides. Active maintenance demonstrated with recent releases in December 2025.

README badges show npm version with link to npmjs.com/package/react.
findings.recommendation: Continue current practices. The changelog is exemplary in providing both high-level summaries and detailed technical changes with migration paths.

findings.description: Flow type annotations used throughout codebase for internal type safety. TypeScript support available via DefinitelyTyped for external consumers.
findings.evidence: Repository tree shows .flowconfig file and flow-typed/ directory indicating Flow type system usage. React is widely known to provide TypeScript definitions through @types/react package on DefinitelyTyped (referenced in package ecosystem).
findings.recommendation: Current dual approach (Flow internally, TypeScript externally) works well. Consider documenting TypeScript support explicitly in README for discoverability.

Each entry includes detailed changes organized by package (React, React DOM, React Server Components) with PR links and author attributions. Total CHANGELOG size: 265,863 bytes.

reasoning: Running the final remaining agent (license) to complete the comprehensive audit of all 5 dimensions before synthesis. License compliance is particularly important for React given its widespread commercial use.

findings.description: Repository contains a complete, valid LICENSE file at the root level declaring MIT License with clear copyright attribution to Meta Platforms, Inc. and affiliates.
findings.evidence:
LICENSE file (SHA: b93be90515ccd0b9daedaa589e42bf5929693f1f) contains full MIT License text: 'MIT License

Copyright (c) Meta Platforms, Inc. and affiliates.

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy of this software...' [1088 bytes total]
findings.recommendation: No action required. LICENSE file meets all requirements for legal clarity and enforceability.

findings.description: The root package.json file does not include a 'license' field, which is a Node.js/npm ecosystem best practice for machine-readable license declaration.
findings.evidence: package.json (SHA: 0ae925a1d4ecd7389a21d953ed05e151c9b03dbc) contains 'private': true, 'workspaces', 'devDependencies', and 'scripts' fields, but no 'license' field declaring the MIT license type.
findings.recommendation: Add '"license": "MIT"' to the root package.json to enable automated license scanning, npm registry metadata, and dependency analysis tools to correctly identify the project license.

findings.description: Source files consistently include comprehensive license headers with copyright notices, license type declaration, and LICENSE file reference.
findings.evidence:
Code search found 55 files matching 'Copyright (c) Meta' in packages/react/src. Sample file packages/react/src/ReactMemo.js (SHA: 8f2c0f53827c95f0002d3dd6d7f10c1327cab744) contains header: '/**
 * Copyright (c) Meta Platforms, Inc. and affiliates.
 *
 * This source code is licensed under the MIT license found in the
 * LICENSE file in the root directory of this source tree.
 *
 * @noflow
 */'
findings.recommendation: Continue maintaining consistent license headers. This practice exceeds MIT License requirements and demonstrates excellent open-source governance.

findings.description: No NOTICE or COPYING files present in the repository root, which is acceptable for MIT-licensed projects.
findings.evidence: Repository tree (SHA: 142cfde89edab3d4eabd6335458b4c8736cebfb6) contains LICENSE, README.md, CHANGELOG.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, SECURITY.md, and MAINTAINERS files, but no NOTICE, COPYING, or ATTRIBUTION files in the 36 root-level entries.
findings.recommendation: No action required. MIT License does not mandate separate NOTICE files. The LICENSE file serves as the sole authoritative legal document. Only add NOTICE if bundling third-party components requiring explicit attribution.

findings.description: Development dependencies appear to use permissive open-source licenses compatible with MIT (signal-based assessment).
findings.evidence: package.json lists 100+ devDependencies including well-known permissive projects: @babel/* packages, eslint ecosystem, jest, rollup, typescript, flow-bin, prettier, and others. No obvious GPL or strong-copyleft dependencies identified in the dependency list.
findings.recommendation: Consider implementing automated license scanning for all direct and transitive dependencies to ensure ongoing compatibility. Current dependency profile shows good license hygiene.

findings.description: MIT License is a highly permissive, well-understood open-source license that maximizes downstream usage rights and minimizes compliance burden.
findings.evidence: LICENSE file clearly states 'MIT License' and includes standard MIT permission grants: 'Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell copies of the Software'
findings.recommendation: No change needed. MIT License is appropriate for a widely-used library like React, enabling maximum adoption across commercial and open-source projects.
